items.title: Australians are dying from a disease that was almost eliminated years ago

children.content: Forty-two babies in Australia died from congenital syphilis between 2011 and 2025, with the outbreak driving health workers into communities to raise awareness of the old-world disease.

image.altText: A man holding a resin mold containing a human skull.

items.title: Fair Work rejects gas giant's claim strikes would harm the economy

children.content: One of Australia's biggest gas producers has lost a bid to stop industrial action at its Darwin facilities after claiming strikes would force shutdowns and cause significant damage to the nation's economy.

image.altText: Evening / dusk shot of Darwin harbour, industrial site, with yellow lights glowing and cranes in view.

items.title: Police charge Tennant Creek teenager with 21-year-old's stabbing murder

children.content: Northern Territory Police charge a 17-year-old boy with the murder of a 21-year-old man who he allegedly stabbed to death in Tennant Creek last week.

gallery.altText: NT Police insignia on mud-splattered car door.

thumbnail.altText: NT Police insignia on mud-splattered car door.

items.title: Multi-billion-dollar economic policy undermined by 'black cladding'

children.content: Almost $15 billion has been spent over a decade via the federal government's Indigenous Procurement Policy, but critics say it's failed to make a significant difference to Indigenous people due to the practice of "black cladding".

image.altText: An Aboriginal flag flying outside of parliament house

items.title: The one of a kind remote NT camp where 'it's cool to go to school'

children.content: Children from across Central Australia have joined a remote Northern Territory camp inside the Watarrka National Park where "it's cool to go to school" — and the footy doesn't hurt either.

image.altText: A child swings a baseball bat on a grassy, dirt field.

items.title: Ireland is a data centre 'cautionary tale'. This is the impact on locals

children.content: As Australia and other countries embark on a new wave of AI-driven data centre development, locals in Ireland share how the runaway growth of the facilities can affect everyday life.

articles.title: AI company set to become Tasmania's biggest power user

children.content: Artificial intelligence company Firmus Technologies will become Tasmania's biggest power user if its ambitions to establish three "AI factories" in the state come to fruition.

image.altText: A high tech room.

items.title: Penny Wong welcomes US–Iran agreement to end hostilities in Middle East

children.content: Foreign Minister Penny Wong says Australia welcomes an agreement reached between the US and Iran to end hostilities in the Middle East. Follow live.

image.altText: Two men and a woman in suits stand at a podium speaking to journalists with the Australian flag in the background.

items.title: Business groups urge rejection of CGT changes

children.content: Business groups are urging the government to scrap its capital gains tax changes as the first tranche of measures is scrutinised at a parliamentary inquiry.

image.altText: Jim Chalmers close up looking serious with Australian flag in background.

children.content: Almost $15 billion has been spent over a decade via the federal government's Indigenous Procurement Policy, but critics say it's failed to make a significant difference to Indigenous people due to the practice of "black cladding".

items.title: Buyer's agency clients urged to proceed days before collapse

children.content: Emails and messages seen by ABC News show Dashdot's staff were still encouraging prospective customers to engage with the buyers agency shortly before it entered voluntary liquidation.

image.altText: Man with a serious expressions sits at a table with a laptop in front of him.

items.title: Travellers owed $4.3 million as dream holidays shattered

children.content: Australian travellers who collectively lost millions after a travel agency collapsed are calling for an overhaul of consumer protections in the sector.

items.title: Twelve killed as skydiving plane goes down shortly after take-off

children.content: The private plane had just taken off and made a turn before it crashed and caught fire, authorities said.

image.altText: A drone view of a crashed plane in a field next to a highway

items.title: Extreme ways young people are trying to secure rentals

children.content: Young Australians are going to extreme lengths to secure rental homes in an over-saturated market, which is leaving "more homeless than ever before".

image.altText: A 24-year-old woman and her dog looking at the camera

items.title: Palestinians fear 'archaeology has become a tool' in Israel's plans for West Bank

children.content: Palestinians believe there is more to Israel's recently announced plans to develop heritage sites along the West Bank, arguing archaeology has "become a tool, a mechanism, to justify settlement and justify the displacement of Palestinians" across the area.

image.altText: A Palestinian flag hangs above Sebastia.

items.title: History quiz: Test yourself on these past events

children.content: When was Salt Lake City awarded the XIX Winter Olympics? And how much do you know about the Fast and the Furious films? Test yourself in this week's history quiz.

image.altText: Frida Kahlo, Mexico, art, artist, anniversary, self-portrait

items.title: We sent six filmmakers to six countries to make a movie. How did they go?

children.content: From dating in Jamaica to world domination in Greenland, catch up on the films from the second week of the new season of Race Around The World.

image.altText: A young child stands on a snow hill.

items.title: 'I fear for my safety': Victim unable to break lease without paying

children.content: Annie feared for her safety from a new next-door neighbour in northern Tasmania, but was told she couldn't break her lease without continuing to pay rent. She was then sexually assaulted.

image.altText: A woman sits on a bench on the foreshore

items.title: Chemicals in Antarctic huts pose 'potential explosive' risk

children.content: Chemicals found inside two of Antarctica's most important historically sites, Earnest Shackleton and Robert Falcon Scott's huts, could pose "potential explosive hazards", a report has warned.

gallery.altText: Shackleton's Nimrod Hut is nestled in the landscape at at Cape Royds, Mount Erebus is shrouded in cloud in the background.

thumbnail.altText: Shackleton's Nimrod Hut is nestled in the landscape at at Cape Royds, Mount Erebus is shrouded in cloud in the background.

items.title: Woman dies in rope jump fall in Brazil

children.content: In videos of the accident circulating online, two men hoist the 21-year-old above their heads and launch her off a bridge in São Paulo.

image.altText: A police car parked in front of a glass building

items.title: Australian dollar rises on US-Iran deal, ASX to lift

children.content: The Australian dollar is rising after Donald Trump confirmed a deal has been reached with Iran, saying the Strait of Hormuz will open. Follow the day's events and insights from our business reporters on the ABC News live markets blog.

image.altText: A trader looks up at a chart on his computer screen while working on the floor of the New York Stock Exchange.

items.title: Concerns new BHP agreement 'locks in' basin water extraction

children.content: Environmentalists and First Nations groups say a new mining agreement with BHP does not do enough to end water extraction from the Great Artesian Basin. But the SA government says the agreement imposes stricter environmental regulation.

image.altText: An overhead view of a BHP wellfield in outback South Australia.

items.title: PFAS is slowly moving towards Wagga's drinking water supply

children.content: Wagga Wagga has two known sites contaminated by "forever chemicals" and some say not enough information about how it is being managed is available.

image.altText: An older man sits in a chair on a verandah surrounded by plants, reading letters.

items.title: Japan to roll out nationwide LGBTQIA+ education for the first time

children.content: The country is taking a big step in its efforts to address gay rights but not everyone is convinced it goes far enough.

items.title: Fraud claims and a $400k sculpture: What a secret RSL report found

children.content: The secret report details claims of the misappropriation of funds and fraud, including one of the NSW charity's sub-branches raising money for veterans from the Korean defence force.

image.altText: Returned & Services League Australia logo on the front doors of the RSL Queensland offices in Brisbane.

items.title: Anglers petition Qld government to relax bull shark fishing rules as numbers rise

children.content: The Queensland government is being petitioned to relax fishing limits for bull sharks and consider culls as anglers and waterway users fear numbers are growing.

image.altText: shark being pushed back out into the river

items.title: UK armed forces intercept Russian 'shadow fleet' tanker

children.content: The British armed forces have intercepted the sanctioned oil tanker in a bid to disrupt the oil revenue ​that helps fund Russia's war in Ukraine.

items.title: Hamilton claims famous Ferrari win, F1 leader suffers late car failure

children.content: Lewis Hamilton has wound back the clock and announced himself as a world championship contender again, claiming his maiden grand prix win as a Ferrari driver.

image.altText: Lewis Hamilton punching the air with delight, holding the winner's trophy

items.title: De Minaur falls short in Dutch ATP final as Wimbledon preparation continues

children.content: Australia's top-ranked men's player Alex de Minaur fights to a tie-break in the Rosmalen Championships final but falls to his Polish opponent.

items.title: Swiss voters set to reject population cap in referendum

children.content: A projection shows voters prioritising economic stability and the country's ties with the European Union over immigration concerns.

image.altText: A billboard sign captured by the side of the road which says: Fed up with traffic jams! No Switzerland with 10 million

items.title: Police officer in custody after Perth girl shot dead overseas

children.content: A nine-year-old Perth girl has been fatally shot by police in Pakistan after officers mistook her family's car for that of suspected criminals.

image.altText: A silhouette of two Pakistani police officers holding rifles at a barrier or gate of some sort.

items.title: Like a 'tin of sardines': The library programs at bursting point on Melbourne's fringe

children.content: Local councils and Public Libraries Victoria say the way public libraries are funded across Victoria needs reform, with communities in Melbourne's growth corridors described as "chronically underfunded".
